question: P.E. is a 56-year-old man who comes to the clinic with a 3-day history of fever, chills, pleuritic chest pain, malaise, and productive cough. In the clinic, his temperature is 102.1°F (38.9°C) (all other vital signs are normal). His chest radiograph reveals consolidation in the right lower lobe. His white blood cell count (WBC) is 14,400 cells/mm³, but all other laboratory values are normal. He is given a diagnosis of community-acquired pneumonia (CAP). He has not received any antibiotics in 5 years and has no chronic disease states. Which is the best empiric therapy for P.E.?

options.text: Cefuroxime axetil 250 mg orally twice daily.
Trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole double strength orally twice daily.

explanation: The patient has CAP that does not require hospitalization (Pneumonia Severity Index [PSI] score is 56, class II, indicating low risk). Since he has no recent antibiotic use (within the past 3 months) and no comorbidities, he is at low risk of drug-resistant S. pneumoniae (DRSP). The drug of choice is either a macrolide or doxycycline. Cefuroxime is not recommended for CAP treatment, fluoroquinolones are only recommended if the patient has had recent antibiotics or comorbidities, and trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole is not used for CAP.

question: H.W. is a 38-year-old woman who presents with a fever, malaise, dry cough, nasal congestion, and severe headaches. Her symptoms began suddenly 3 days ago, and she has been in bed since then. She reports no other illness in her family, but several people have recently called in sick at work. It is influenza season. Which is best for H.W.?

options.text: Azithromycin 500 mg, followed by 250 mg daily orally for 4 more days.
Amoxicillin clavulanic acid 75 mg orally twice daily for 5 days.
Oseltamivir 75 mg twice daily orally for 5 days.

explanation: The patient’s symptoms (high temperature, malaise, dry cough, nasal congestion, and severe headaches) are highly consistent with influenza. Antibacterial agents like Azithromycin (A) or Amoxicillin/clavulanic acid (B) would not affect recovery because the etiology is viral. Oseltamivir (C) should be initiated within 48 hours of symptom onset; since her symptoms began 3 days ago, it will not affect recovery. Therefore, symptomatic treatment is indicated.

question: A study is designed to assess the risk of pneumococcal pneumonia in older adults 10 years or more after receiving their last pneumococcal vaccination, compared with older adults who have never received any pneumococcal vaccinations. Which study design is best?
explanation: A case-control study is the most appropriate, ethical, cost-effective, and timely study design for this question. A prospective cohort study or a randomized controlled trial would be too costly and time-consuming due to the low incidence of confirmed pneumococcal pneumonia and the required 10-year observation period. A randomized controlled trial would also be unethical because patients would have to be randomly assigned to no vaccination. A case series would not provide comparative data.

question: A.B. is a 63-year-old woman who presents to the emergency department with left leg pain and erythema. The pain and erythema have worsened over the past 24 hours. The left leg is significantly swollen with a large area of erythema and large bullae extending from the thigh to the upper leg. There is crepitus within the soft tissue. A.B. is found to have rapidly progressing necrotizing fasciitis. A.B. has normal renal function and no known drug allergies. Which is the best empiric therapy for A.B.?

options.text: Vancomycin 15 mg/kg intravenously every 12 hours plus meropenem 1 g intravenously every 8 hours plus clindamycin 900 mg intravenously every 8 hours.
Linezolid 600 mg intravenously every 12 hours plus ceftriaxone 1 g intravenously every 24 hours plus azithromycin 500 mg intravenously daily.

explanation: Necrotizing fasciitis (NF) is often a mixed infection involving gram-positive, gram-negative, and anaerobic bacteria, and requires urgent surgical debridement plus broad-spectrum empiric antibiotics. The regimen must include activity against P. aeruginosa (often required in empiric NF therapy) and clindamycin or linezolid to suppress toxin and cytokine production by Streptococcus or Clostridium spp.. Option C (Vancomycin + Meropenem + Clindamycin) is the best empiric option as it covers MRSA (Vancomycin), broad Gram-negative/anaerobic organisms (Meropenem, a carbapenem), and includes Clindamycin for toxin suppression.

question: N.R. is a 28-year-old woman who presents to the clinic with a 2-day history of dysuria, frequency, and urgency. She has no significant medical history, and the only drug she takes is an oral contraceptive. Which is the best empiric therapy for N.R.?

options.text: Nitrofurantoin extended release (ER) 100 mg orally twice daily for 3 days.
Ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally twice daily for 7 days.
Trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole double strength orally twice daily for 3 days.
Cephalexin 500 mg orally four times daily for 3 days.

explanation: The patient has uncomplicated cystitis. Trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole (TMP/SMX) double strength twice daily orally for 3 days is the recommended therapeutic regimen for uncomplicated cystitis. Nitrofurantoin is first-line, but the recommended duration is 5 days, not 3 days. Fluoroquinolones (like ciprofloxacin) should be avoided as first-line therapy when other options are available. Beta-lactams are less effective and data is limited on their 3-day use.

question: B.Y. is an 85-year-old woman who is bedridden and lives in a nursing home. She is chronically catheterized, and her urinary catheter was last changed 3 weeks ago. Today, her urine is cloudy, and a urinalysis reveals many bacteria. B.Y. is not noticing any symptoms. A urine culture is obtained. Which option is best for B.Y.?

options.text: No antibiotic therapy because she is chronically catheterized and has no symptoms.
No antibiotic therapy, but the catheter should be changed.
Ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally twice daily for 7 days and change the catheter.
Ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally twice daily for 14–21 days without a change in catheter.

explanation: For an asymptomatic patient who is chronically catheterized and exhibits bacteriuria, no antibiotic therapy is indicated. Nearly all patients with long-term indwelling catheters will be bacteriuric. Treating asymptomatic bacteriuria only increases the risk of acquiring resistant organisms.

question: V.E. is a 44-year-old man who presents to the emergency department with a warm, erythematous, and painful right lower extremity. There is no raised border at the edge of the infection. Three days ago, he scratched his leg on a barbed wire fence on his property (no puncture wound associated with the fence). His temperature has been as high as 101.8°F (38°C) with chills. Doppler studies of his lower extremity are negative. Blood cultures are negative. Which is the best empiric therapy for V.E.?

options.text: Penicillin G 2 million units intravenously every 4 hours.

explanation: The patient appears to have acute cellulitis, characterized by non-elevated, poorly defined margins. Cellulitis is usually caused by Streptococcus or Staphylococcus. Cefazolin (a first-generation cephalosporin) is the drug of choice. Penicillin G (B) is the treatment of choice for erysipelas, but the lack of a raised border suggests cellulitis. Piperacillin/tazobactam (C) is too broad spectrum for acute cellulitis. Oral antibiotics like doxycycline (D) are inappropriate when the patient is febrile with extensive cellulitis.

question: R.K. is a 36-year-old woman who presents to the emergency department with a severe headache and neck stiffness. Her temperature is 37.5°C. After a negative computed tomographic scan of the head, a lumbar puncture reveals the following: glucose 54 mg/dL (peripheral, 104 mg/dL), protein 88 mg/dL, and WBC 220 cells/mm³ (100% lymphocytes). The Gram stain reveals no organisms. Which option is best for R.K.?

options.text: This is aseptic meningitis, and no antibiotics are necessary.
Administer ceftriaxone 2 g intravenously every 12 hours until the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) cultures are negative for bacteria.
Administer ceftriaxone 2 g intravenously every 12 hours and vancomycin 15 mg/kg intravenously every 12 hours until the CSF cultures are negative for bacteria.
Administer acyclovir 500 mg intravenously every 8 hours until the CSF culture results are negative for bacteria.

explanation: In adults (2–50 years) presenting with symptoms suggestive of bacterial meningitis (headache, neck stiffness, slightly elevated temperature, although the CSF findings, particularly the predominance of lymphocytes and relatively high CSF glucose compared to typical bacterial meningitis, might suggest aseptic/viral meningitis), antibiotics must be given until CSF cultures are definitively negative. Empiric therapy for bacterial meningitis in adults should include ceftriaxone in combination with vancomycin. Vancomycin is necessary for activity against resistant S. pneumoniae.

question: L.G. is a 49-year-old woman with a history of mitral valve prolapse. She presents to her physician's office with malaise and a low-grade fever. Her physician notes that her murmur is louder than usual and orders blood cultures and an echocardiogram. A large vegetation is observed on L.G.’s mitral valve, and her blood cultures are growing Enterococcus faecalis (susceptible to all antibiotics). Which is the best therapy for L.G.?

options.text: Penicillin G plus gentamicin for 2 weeks.

explanation: Enterococcal endocarditis should be treated for 4–6 weeks. The 2-week treatment regimen is indicated only for streptococcal endocarditis. Since the organism is susceptible to all antibiotics, Ampicillin plus Ceftriaxone for 6 weeks is a recommended regimen for penicillin-susceptible enterococcal endocarditis. Cefazolin has no activity against Enterococcus.

question: N.L. is a 27-year-old woman with no significant medical history. She reports to the emergency department with fever and severe right lower quadrant abdominal pain. The pain had been dull for the past few days, but it suddenly became severe during the past 8 hours. Her temperature is 103.5°F (39.7°C), and she has rebound tenderness on abdominal examination. She is taken to surgery immediately, where a perforated appendix is diagnosed and repaired. Which is the best follow-up antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Vancomycin 1000 mg intravenously every 12 hours plus metronidazole 500 mg intravenously every 8 hours.
Cefazolin 1 g intravenously every 8 hours plus ciprofloxacin 400 mg intravenously every 12 hours.
Ceftriaxone 1 g intravenously every 24 hours plus metronidazole 500 mg intravenously every 8 hours.
No antibiotics needed after surgical repair of a perforated appendix.

explanation: A perforated appendix requires antibiotics after surgery for an intra-abdominal infection. The combination of Vancomycin and Metronidazole (A) lacks adequate activity against aerobic, Gram-negative organisms (e.g., E. coli). The combination of Cefazolin and Ciprofloxacin (B) lacks adequate activity against anaerobic organisms (e.g., B. fragilis group). Ceftriaxone plus Metronidazole (C) is a good choice for intra-abdominal infections, although it has limited activity against Enterococcus.

question: O.R. is a 73-year-old man who presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of fever, chills, frequency, urgency, and perineal pain. A urinalysis reveals many bacteria. A rectal examination reveals a swollen, tender prostate. He is given a diagnosis of acute bacterial prostatitis. Which is the best regimen for this patient?

options.text: Amoxicillin clavulanate 75 orally twice daily for 14 days.
Trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole double strength orally twice daily for 7 days.
Cefprozil 500 mg orally twice daily for 21 days.
Ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally twice daily for 28 days.

explanation: Beta-lactam antibiotics (A, C) are generally not recommended as first-line agents for acute bacterial prostatitis. Although TMP/SMX is appropriate, the 7-day duration (B) is too short; treatment duration should be 4 weeks (28 days). Fluoroquinolones (Ciprofloxacin) are an appropriate antibiotic class for prostatitis, and the 28-day duration is also appropriate.

question: J.M. is a 72-year-old woman with a history of atrial fibrillation, hypertension, a right total hip replacement 8 months earlier, and Crohn disease. She has no drug allergies. She presents to the hospital with increasing pain in her prosthetic hip over the past month. There is concern about hip osteomyelitis. Bone cultures are growing methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus. J.M. has normal renal function and no known drug allergies. Which is the best antibiotic regimen for this patient?

options.text: Vancomycin 1000 mg intravenously every 12 hours plus rifampin 300 mg orally twice daily for 2 weeks.
Cefazolin 2 g intravenously every 8 hours plus rifampin 300 mg orally twice daily for 6 weeks followed by long-term oral antibiotics.
Nafcillin 1 g intravenously every 4 hours for 6 weeks.
Daptomycin 6 mg/kg intravenously daily for 6 weeks followed by long-term oral antibiotics.

explanation: Since the infection is caused by Methicillin-Sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA), using Vancomycin (A) or Daptomycin (D) is unnecessary and poses a risk of toxicity. For prosthetic joint infections caused by Staphylococcus that retain the prosthesis, the treatment involves a pathogen-specific intravenous therapy (Nafcillin or Cefazolin) combined with rifampin for 2–6 weeks, followed by rifampin plus a fluoroquinolone for 3 to 6 months. Option C is incorrect because rifampin must be combined with the primary antibiotic for prosthetic joint infections. Cefazolin plus Rifampin for 6 weeks followed by long-term oral antibiotics (B) is the best choice.

question: B.K. is a 58-year-old woman (height 66 inches, weight 82 kg) who is scheduled to undergo a total knee replacement tomorrow. She has no significant medical history and no drug allergies. Which is the best surgical prophylaxis regimen for this patient?

options.text: Cefazolin 2 g within 1 hour of the incision and no doses postoperatively.
Cefazolin 2 g within 4 hours of the incision and three doses every 8 hours postoperatively.
Cefazolin 1 g within 1 hour of the incision and three doses every 8 hours postoperatively.
Cefazolin 1 g within 4 hours of the incision and no doses postoperatively.

explanation: For orthopedic procedures involving prosthetic materials, surgical prophylaxis is necessary. The preferred agent is Cefazolin at a dosage of at least 2 g (or 3 g for patients weighing 120 kg or more). The antibiotic must be administered within 1 hour of the incision. Administration beyond surgical closure is generally unnecessary, especially for shorter orthopedic procedures.

question: R.L. is a 68-year-old man who presents to the emergency department with coughing and shortness of breath... If R.L. were hospitalized, which would be the best empiric therapy for him?

options.text: Piperacillin/tazobactam 4.5 g intravenously every 6 hours plus gentamicin 180 mg intravenously every 12 hours.
Ceftriaxone 2 g intravenously every 24 hours plus azithromycin 500 mg intravenously every 24 hours.

explanation: For hospitalized patients with non-severe CAP (R.L. has risk factors like CHF, requiring inpatient management), empiric treatment involves a respiratory fluoroquinolone or a beta-lactam plus a macrolide (or doxycycline). Ceftriaxone plus azithromycin is the best initial choice because it provides excellent activity against typical organisms (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae) and atypical organisms (due to azithromycin). Ampicillin/sulbactam (A) and Piperacillin/tazobactam + Gentamicin (B) lack coverage for atypical organisms. Doxycycline monotherapy (D) is not recommended for hospitalized patients.

question: B.P. is a 66-year-old woman who underwent a two-vessel coronary artery bypass graft 8 days ago and has been on a ventilator... Which is the best empiric therapy for B.P.?

options.text: Ceftriaxone 2 g intravenously every 24 hours plus gentamicin 7 mg/kg intravenously every 24 hours plus linezolid 600 mg intravenously every 12 hours.
Levofloxacin 750 mg intravenously every 24 hours plus linezolid 600 mg intravenously every 12 hours.
Cefepime 2 g intravenously every 8 hours plus tobramycin 7 mg/kg intravenously every 24 hours plus vancomycin 15 mg/kg intravenously every 12 hours.

explanation: B.P. has Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia (VAP) since it arose more than 48 hours after intubation. Because she has been hospitalized for 5 days or more (8 days), she is at increased risk for Multi-Drug Resistant (MDR) organisms, specifically P. aeruginosa and MRSA. Empiric therapy for HAP/VAP should cover S. aureus (MSSA), P. aeruginosa, and other Gram-negative organisms. Due to her risk factors for MDR organisms, she needs two antibiotics with activity against P. aeruginosa and one agent for MRSA. Option D (Cefepime + Tobramycin + Vancomycin) provides two antipseudomonal agents (Cefepime and Tobramycin) and coverage for MRSA (Vancomycin). Ceftriaxone (A) is incorrect because it lacks P. aeruginosa activity.

question: S.C. is a 46-year-old woman who presents to the clinic with purulent nasal discharge, nasal and facial congestion, headaches, fever, and dental pain. Her symptoms began about 10 days ago, improved after about 4 days, and then worsened again a few days later. Which is the best empiric therapy for S.C.?

options.text: Cefpodoxime proxetil 200 mg orally twice daily.
Clindamycin 300 mg orally four times daily.
Amoxicillin/clavulanate 875 mg/125 mg orally twice daily.

explanation: The patient’s symptoms (lasting >10 days, or having improved and then worsened—the “double-sickening” pattern) suggest acute bacterial sinusitis. Amoxicillin/clavulanate is considered a first-line agent for bacterial sinusitis. No antibiotic therapy (D) is incorrect because the pattern suggests bacterial infection. Cefpodoxime or Clindamycin alone are not recommended for treatment.

question: G.N. is a 62-year-old woman who presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of urinary frequency and dysuria... Which is the best empiric therapy for G.N.?

options.text: Trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole double strength orally twice daily for 7 days. Monitor INR carefully.
Ciprofloxacin 400 mg intravenously twice daily and then 500 mg orally twice daily for a total of 7 days. Monitor INR carefully.
Gentamicin 140 mg intravenously every 24 hours for 3 days.
Tigecycline 100 mg once, then 50 mg intravenously every 12 hours and then doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for a total of 10 days.

explanation: G.N. presents with symptoms and signs (fever, flank pain, abnormal vital signs, positive casts on urinalysis) consistent with complicated pyelonephritis. TMP/SMX (A) is inappropriate because the duration is too short (should be 14 days), and it has a significant drug interaction with warfarin. Ciprofloxacin for a total of 7 days is an appropriate regimen and duration for complicated pyelonephritis. Gentamicin for 3 days (C) is too short. Tigecycline/Doxycycline (D) is not recommended for complicated pyelonephritis.

question: G.N. returns to the clinic in 6 months with no urinary symptoms, but her chief concern is now an ulcer on her right foot... Which is the best empiric therapy for G.N.?

options.text: Nafcillin 2 g intravenously every 6 hours for 6–12 weeks.
Tobramycin 120 mg intravenously every 12 hours plus levofloxacin 750 mg intravenously every 24 hours for 1–2 weeks.
Piperacillin/tazobactam 3.375 g intravenously every 6 hours for 1–2 weeks.
Below-the-knee amputation followed by ceftriaxone 1 g intravenously every 24 hours for 1 week.

explanation: G.N. has a diabetic foot infection, which are generally polymicrobial and can involve Gram-positive, Gram-negative, and anaerobic organisms. Given the macerated ulcer and history of water exposure (walking barefoot on the beach, suggesting potential P. aeruginosa risk), a regimen active against P. aeruginosa is likely necessary. Beta-lactamase inhibitor combinations, such as Piperacillin/tazobactam, are good agents because they cover the usual mix of organisms. Nafcillin (A) misses Gram-negative organisms and anaerobes. Tobramycin/Levofloxacin (B) misses anaerobes, and Tobramycin is risky in diabetic patients due to nephrotoxicity.

question: W.A. is a 55-year-old man who is admitted with weight loss, malaise, and severe back pain... A computed tomography guided bone biopsy reveals gram-positive cocci in clusters. Which is the best therapy for W.A.?

options.text: Vancomycin 15 mg/kg intravenously every 12 hours for 6 weeks.
Nafcillin 2 g intravenously every 6 hours for 2 weeks.
Levofloxacin 750 mg orally every 24 hours for 6 weeks.
Ampicillin/sulbactam 3 g intravenously every 6 hours for 2 weeks.

explanation: The patient has findings consistent with vertebral osteomyelitis (bone destruction of lumbar vertebrae L1 and L2). The bone biopsy shows Gram-positive cocci in clusters, likely S. aureus. Since sensitivities are unknown, Vancomycin is the best empiric choice to cover potential MRSA. Treatment duration for acute osteomyelitis is typically 3–6 weeks (average 6 weeks). Options B and D are too short (2 weeks). Oral Levofloxacin (C) is unlikely to achieve adequate bone concentrations to eradicate S. aureus.

question: D.M. is a 21-year-old university student who presents to the emergency department with the worst headache of his life... Which is the best empiric therapy for D.M.?

options.text: Penicillin G 4 million units intravenously every 4 hours.
Ceftriaxone 2 g intravenously every 12 hours plus dexamethasone 10 mg intravenously every 6 hours.
Ceftriaxone 2 g intravenously every 12 hours plus vancomycin 1000 mg intravenously every 8 hours plus dexamethasone 10 mg intravenously every 6 hours.

explanation: D.M. presents with classic bacterial meningitis signs (fever, severe headache, neck stiffness, highly elevated WBC in CSF, low CSF glucose ratio to peripheral glucose). Empiric therapy for bacterial meningitis in children and adults (2–50 years) is Ceftriaxone plus Vancomycin. Vancomycin is crucial for activity against highly penicillin-resistant S. pneumoniae. Dexamethasone should also be added adjunctive therapy because it improves outcomes and decreases mortality in adults with pneumococcal meningitis.

question: D.M.’s CSF cultures grew N. meningitidis. Which is the best recommendation for meningitis prophylaxis?

options.text: The health care providers in close contact with D.M. should receive rifampin 600 mg orally every 12 hours for four doses.
Everyone in D.M.’s dormitory and in all of his classes should receive rifampin 600 mg orally every 24 hours for 4 days.
Everyone in the emergency department at the time of D.M.’s presentation should receive the meningococcal conjugate vaccine.
Everyone in the emergency department at the time of D.M.’s presentation should receive rifampin 600 mg orally every 12 hours for four doses.

explanation: Prophylaxis for N. meningitidis is recommended only for close contacts (household, day care) and those exposed to the index case’s oral secretions. Health care providers who were in close contact (e.g., during intubation or resuscitation) should receive prophylaxis. The correct regimen for adults is Rifampin 600 mg every 12 hours for four doses.

question: T.S. is a 48-year-old man who presents to the emergency department with fever, chills... A transesophageal echocardiogram reveals vegetation on the mitral valve. Which is the best therapeutic regimen for T.S.?

options.text: Nafcillin plus rifampin plus gentamicin therapy for 6 weeks or longer.
Nafcillin plus gentamicin therapy for 2 weeks of both antibiotics.

explanation: T.S. has native valve endocarditis caused by MSSA (S. aureus confirmed by culture). The recommended treatment for MSSA native valve endocarditis is Nafcillin (or Oxacillin or Cefazolin) for 6 weeks. Options A and C are incorrect because the duration (7–10 days or 2 weeks) is too short for S. aureus endocarditis. Option B is incorrect because Rifampin and Gentamicin are only necessary for prosthetic valve endocarditis, not native valve endocarditis.

question: Six months after treatment of his endocarditis, T.S. is visiting his dentist for a tooth extraction. Which antibiotic is best for prophylaxis?

options.text: Tooth extractions do not warrant endocarditis prophylaxis.
Administer amoxicillin 2 g 1 hour before the extraction.
Administer amoxicillin 3 g 1 hour before the extraction and 1.5 g every 6 hours for four doses after the extraction.
T.S. is not at increased risk of endocarditis and does not need prophylactic antibiotics.

explanation: The patient is at increased risk of endocarditis due to his history of the disease. Dental procedures involving perforation of the oral mucosa (like a tooth extraction) warrant prophylaxis for those at risk. The current recommended dose for standard general prophylaxis is Amoxicillin 2 g 1 hour before the procedure.

question: R.K. is a 72-year-old man who presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of redness and swelling of his upper right extremity... Two weeks after completing therapy for his cellulitis, R.K. has watery diarrhea. R.K. goes to the emergency department, and his C. difficile toxin is positive. His WBC is 24,500 cells/mm³, albumin is 2.8 g/dL, and SCr is 1.74 mg/dL (normally around 0.80 mg/dL). Which is the best therapeutic regimen for R.K.?

options.text: Metronidazole 500 mg orally three times daily for 7 days.
Vancomycin 125 mg orally four times daily for 10 days.
Fidaxomicin 200 mg orally twice daily for 14 days.
Rifaximin 400 mg orally twice daily for 7 days.

explanation: R.K. is experiencing a severe C. difficile infection (CDI), indicated by his high WBC (24,500 cells/mm³ > 15 x 10³ cells/mm³) and elevated SCr (1.74 mg/dL > 1.5 mg/dL). For severe CDI, the preferred first-line treatment options are Vancomycin 125 mg orally four times daily for 10 days or Fidaxomicin 200 mg orally twice daily for 10 days. Option C is incorrect because the duration (14 days) is too long. Metronidazole (A) is not optimal for severe infection. Vancomycin 125 mg orally four times daily for 10 days (B) is the best choice among the options.

question: You are a pharmacist who works closely with the surgery department... Which is the best practice for optimizing surgical prophylaxis?

options.text: Antibiotics should be redosed for extended surgical procedures; redose if the surgery lasts longer than 4 hours or involves considerable blood loss.
All patients should be given antibiotics for 24 hours after the procedure; this will optimize prophylaxis.
Preoperative antibiotics can be given up to 4 hours before the incision; this will make giving the antibiotics logistically easier.
Vancomycin is the antibiotic of choice for surgical wound prophylaxis because of its long half-life and activity against MRSA.

explanation: Redosing antibiotics is critical for surgical prophylaxis, especially if the surgery lasts longer than 4 hours (or more than two drug half-lives) or involves considerable blood loss. Giving antibiotics for 24 hours post-procedure (B) is generally unnecessary (except sometimes for cardiac procedures) and increases the risk of resistance. Antibiotics should be administered within 60 minutes before the incision (or 60–120 minutes for Vancomycin), not up to 4 hours before. Vancomycin (D) should not be used routinely for surgical prophylaxis unless the patient has a life-threatening allergy to beta-lactam antibiotics.

question: K.E. is a 29-year-old asymptomatic patient who is HIV positive. She recently found out she is pregnant and is estimated to be early in her first trimester. Her most recent CD4 count was 170 cells/mm³, and her viral load was 100,000 copies/mL... Which is the best therapy for K.E. to prevent HIV transmission to her child?

options.text: No drug therapy is needed; the risks to the fetus outweigh any benefits.
Administer zidovudine 300 mg twice daily orally throughout the pregnancy, followed by zidovudine during labor and subsequently to the infant for 6 weeks.
No drug therapy is needed now, but administer a single dose of nevirapine at the onset of labor.
Administer a potent combination antiretroviral therapy (ART) regimen.

explanation: A potent combination ART regimen should be initiated in all HIV-infected patients, including pregnant women, regardless of the CD4 count. Combination ART regimens are preferred over single-drug therapy (B) because they significantly decrease the risk of transmission. Since she has a high viral load and low CD4 count, combination ART is necessary.

question: R.E. is a 33-year-old man who recently tested HIV positive. He is initiated on tenofovir alafenamide, emtricitabine, and dolutegravir. Which is the best counseling for R.E.?

options.text: Watch for trouble sleeping because dolutegravir can cause insomnia.
If you think you are having a drug-related adverse effect, cut the dose of all of your drugs in half.
Talk to your pharmacist about drug interactions because both dolutegravir and tenofovir inhibit cytochrome P450 (CYP) 3A4.
Tenofovir and emtricitabine cause additive peripheral neuropathy, so let your pharmacist know if you have tingling in your extremities.

explanation: Dolutegravir is associated with insomnia and CNS symptoms. Instructing the patient to cut the dose in half (B) is incorrect because antiretroviral drugs should always be used at the recommended dose. Neither dolutegravir nor tenofovir inhibit CYP3A4. Peripheral neuropathy (D) is not associated with tenofovir or emtricitabine.

question: P.P., a 43-year-old man who is HIV positive, presents to the clinic with a headache that has gradually worsened during the past 2 weeks... He is given a diagnosis of cryptococcal meningitis and successfully treated. Which is the best follow-up therapy for P.P.?

options.text: Administer amphotericin B 1 mg/kg weekly intravenously.
He is protected as long as he is also receiving Pneumocystis jiroveci pneumonia (PJP) prophylaxis.

explanation: Patients with cryptococcal meningitis require long-term secondary prevention/maintenance therapy. The preferred maintenance therapy is Fluconazole 200 mg daily for at least 1 year. Fluconazole is preferred over weekly intravenous Amphotericin B due to its ease of administration and lower toxicity. PJP prophylaxis agents have no activity against Cryptococcus.

question: A study compares the incidence of active tuberculosis (TB) infection in patients receiving isoniazid versus rifampin for latent TB infection. After completing therapy (6 months for isoniazid and 4 months for rifampin), 0.3% in the isoniazid group and 0.8% in the rifampin group progress to active disease. Which best represents how many patients would need to be treated with isoniazid over rifampin to prevent one progression to active disease?
explanation: The Number Needed to Treat (NNT) is calculated as 1 / Absolute Risk Reduction (ARR). ARR = Incidence in Rifampin group (0.008) - Incidence in Isoniazid group (0.003) = 0.005. NNT = 1 / 0.005 = 200.

question: G.T. is a 34-year-old woman positive for HIV who is brought to the emergency department by her boyfriend... A computed tomographic scan shows two large ring-enhancing lesions in her brain indicating toxoplasmic encephalitis. Her most recent CD4 count was 85 cells/mm³... Which is the best therapy for G.T.?

options.text: High-dose trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole plus clindamycin for 6 weeks.
Pyrimethamine plus clindamycin and leucovorin for 6 weeks.

explanation: The patient has toxoplasmic encephalitis. Pyrimethamine plus sulfadiazine is the preferred therapy. However, leucovorin (folinic acid) must always be added to pyrimethamine to reduce its bone marrow–suppressive effects. Since Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine + Leucovorin is not an option, the alternative regimen of Pyrimethamine plus Clindamycin plus Leucovorin for 6 weeks is the correct choice, especially since the patient already takes dapsone for PJP prophylaxis, suggesting a potential sulfa sensitivity.

question: H.Y., a 49-year-old man with acute myeloid leukemia, is given a diagnosis of tuberculosis (TB) and initiated on empiric therapy with rifampin, isoniazid, ethambutol, and pyrazinamide. Three months into his TB therapy, he is hospitalized and given a diagnosis of aspergillosis; treatment is needed in addition to his TB treatment. Which is the best antifungal to use in H.Y.?
explanation: The patient needs treatment for Aspergillosis while simultaneously receiving Rifampin for TB. Fluconazole (A) has no activity against Aspergillus. Voriconazole (B) and Isavuconazonium (C) both have significant drug interactions with Rifampin, which is a strong enzyme inducer, leading to significantly lower antifungal concentrations. Amphotericin lipid formulation (D) is the best option because it is active against Aspergillus and lacks drug interactions with the TB medications.

question: P.I. is a 35-year-old woman who presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of night sweats, fatigue, weight loss, and a persistent cough... Two days later, her TST is measured at 20-mm induration, and her sputum sample is positive for acid-fast bacilli. P.I., who has no pertinent medical history, has never been outside the United States. Which regimen is the best therapy for P.I.?

options.text: Isoniazid, rifampin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol for 2 months, followed by isoniazid and rifampin for 4 more months.
Levofloxacin, isoniazid, and rifampin for both TB and other bacterial causes of pneumonia.

explanation: P.I. has active tuberculosis (TB), confirmed by symptoms, positive TST, and positive acid-fast bacilli sputum sample. The recommended therapy for drug-susceptible active TB in patients without HIV is an intensive phase of four drugs: Isoniazid, Rifampin, Pyrazinamide, and Ethambutol for 2 months (daily or five times weekly), followed by a continuation phase of Isoniazid and Rifampin for 4 more months. Therapy with fewer than three antibiotics in the intensive phase (A, C) is inappropriate. Fluoroquinolones (D) are not recommended as first-line monotherapy.

question: A prospective, double-blind study compared the effects of two therapies... Which is the best statistical test to use to compare end points such as the mean change in viral load or mean change in CD4 counts?
explanation: The end points (mean change in viral load, mean change in CD4 counts) are continuous data and, given the large sample size (350 patients), are assumed to be normally distributed. Therefore, a parametric test is indicated. The Student t-test is the best parametric test for comparing two groups. Analysis of variance (A) compares more than two groups, and the Chi-square test (B) compares categorical data.

question: J.Z. is a 42-year-old HIV-positive man with a diagnosis of cytomegalovirus (CMV) retinitis 9 months ago... Today, his CD4 count is 312 cells/mm³. Which is the best recommendation for valganciclovir therapy in J.Z.?

options.text: Discontinue valganciclovir because his CD4 count has been above 100 cells/mm³ for 3 months.
Continue valganciclovir until the CD4 count has been above 100 cells/mm³ for 1 year.
Finish 1 year of therapy with valganciclovir and then discontinue the medication if his CD4 count is still above 100 cells/mm³.

explanation: Secondary prophylaxis (chronic maintenance therapy) for CMV retinitis may be discontinued if the CD4 count is greater than 100 cells/mm³ for 3–6 months in response to ART, and the CMV lesions are inactive. Since his CD4 count (175, 312) has been above 100 cells/mm³ for at least 3 months (3 months ago to today), Valganciclovir can be discontinued (in consultation with an ophthalmologist).

question: K.T. is a 55-year-old woman who is hospitalized with a presumed Candida infection. She currently receives simvastatin, diazepam, lisinopril, dofetilide, and metoprolol. Which antifungal would be best to use empirically with the other medications K.T. is receiving?
explanation: Empiric treatment for Candidemia should start with an echinocandin (like Micafungin). Fluconazole should only be used if the organism is known or suspected to be sensitive to fluconazole. Micafungin is preferred empirically because echinocandins typically have fewer drug interactions compared to azoles, which is important given her polypharmacy (including dofetilide and statins, which are susceptible to CYP interactions). Liposomal amphotericin B (A) is generally reserved for resistance or intolerance.

question: T.E. is a 51-year-old man who has been HIV positive for 23 years... He is allergic to penicillins and sulfonamide antibiotics. Which regimen is the best PJP prophylaxis for T.E.?

options.text: Trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole one double-strength tablet by mouth daily.
Pentamidine 300 mg by nebulization once monthly.

explanation: T.E. needs primary prophylaxis for PJP because his CD4 count is 112 cells/mm³ (< 200 cells/mm³). Due to his sulfonamide allergy, TMP/SMX (A) is contraindicated. Dapsone (B) is an alternative, but the dose listed (weekly) is incorrect; when used alone, Dapsone must be dosed daily. Pentamidine by nebulization (D) is difficult to administer. Atovaquone 1500 mg once daily is considered the preferred alternative prophylactic regimen based on safety, tolerability, and efficacy.

question: O.D. is a 38-year-old HIV positive man originally from Puerto Rico... A bronchoscopy is performed and the cultures are positive for Histoplasma capsulatum. What is the best treatment for O.D.?

options.text: Amphotericin B lipid formulation for 1–2 weeks followed by itraconazole 200 mg three times daily for 3 days followed by 200 mg twice daily for 12 weeks total.
Micafungin 100 mg IV daily for 1–2 weeks followed by fluconazole 400 mg daily for 12 weeks total.
Voriconazole 200 mg twice daily for 6 weeks total.
Isavuconazole 200 mg every 8 hours for 2 days followed by 200 mg daily for 6 weeks total.

explanation: Given that O.D. is hospitalized, worsening, and HIV-positive, this is considered a moderate to severe case of Histoplasmosis. The recommended treatment is the induction phase using a Lipid formulation of Amphotericin B (3–5 mg/kg/day) for 1–2 weeks, followed by the consolidation phase using Itraconazole to complete 12 weeks of therapy. Echinocandins (B) are generally ineffective against Histoplasma. Voriconazole (C) and Isavuconazole (D) are not currently recommended due to limited clinical data.

question: R.V. is a 35-year-old man who presents to the emergency department with a persistent cough... He is not HIV-infected. Which one of the following would be the best empiric therapy for R.V.?

options.text: Rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol 3 times weekly for 2 months followed by rifampin and isoniazid 2 times weekly for 4 months.
Rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol daily for 2 months followed by rifampin and isoniazid 5 times weekly for 4 months.
Rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol daily for 2 months followed by rifampin, isoniazid, and ethambutol 3 times weekly for 4 months.
Rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol 5 times weekly for 2 months followed by rifampin, isoniazid, and pyrazinamide 2 times weekly for 4 months.

explanation: This patient has active TB confirmed by symptoms, positive IGRA, and positive acid-fast bacillus sputum. The standard recommended regimen for active TB in patients without HIV involves an intensive phase of four drugs for 2 months, followed by a continuation phase of two drugs (Rifampin and Isoniazid) for 4 months. The ideal schedule is daily or 5 times weekly for both phases. Option A (3 times weekly/2 times weekly) is optional but not the recommended therapy. Option B reflects the daily/5 times weekly schedule. C and D include three drugs in the continuation phase, which is incorrect

question: A 68-year-old man with a history of COPD and diabetes is diagnosed with community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) in an outpatient setting. He has not taken antibiotics recently. According to the guidelines, which treatment would be appropriate for him?
explanation: Patients with COPD and diabetes have comorbidities; respiratory fluoroquinolone is recommended per IDSA/ATS CAP guidelines for outpatients with comorbidities.

question: A patient in the intensive care unit for 10 days develops ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP). The hospital unit has a 15% incidence of MRSA. What is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic strategy for this patient?

options.text: A standard CAP regimen such as ceftriaxone plus azithromycin
A single broad-spectrum agent such as piperacillin/tazobactam
Two agents with anti-pseudomonal activity plus an agent with MRSA activity
An anti-pseudomonal β-lactam plus an aminoglycoside

explanation: VAP with 15% MRSA incidence (>10%) requires two anti-pseudomonal agents plus MRSA coverage per IDSA/SHEA HAP/VAP guidelines.

question: A 45-year-old man presents with symptoms of influenza that began suddenly 60 hours ago. He is otherwise healthy with no chronic conditions. What is the most appropriate course of action?

options.text: Prescribe baloxavir as a single dose
Prescribe azithromycin to prevent secondary bacterial pneumonia

explanation: Neuraminidase inhibitors must be started within 48 hours of symptom onset to be effective; at 60 hours, only symptomatic treatment is appropriate.

question: In which of the following patient populations is it recommended to screen for and treat asymptomatic bacteriuria?

options.text: Elderly patients in nursing homes with indwelling catheters

explanation: Pregnant women are the recommended population for screening and treatment of asymptomatic bacteriuria per IDSA guidelines.

question: A patient is diagnosed with necrotizing fasciitis. In addition to broad-spectrum coverage against Gram-positive, Gram-negative, and anaerobic bacteria, which adjunctive antibiotic is recommended empirically to suppress toxin and cytokine production if group A streptococci are suspected?
explanation: Clindamycin (or linezolid) is added empirically to suppress toxin and cytokine production when Group A Streptococcus is suspected in necrotizing fasciitis.

question: A patient with a prosthetic hip infection caused by methicillin-sensitive *Staphylococcus aureus* (MSSA) undergoes debridement with retention of the prosthesis. What is the recommended antibiotic strategy following the initial intravenous therapy?

options.text: A prolonged course of rifampin combined with an oral fluoroquinolone
A 2-week course of intravenous vancomycin plus rifampin
Intravenous nafcillin for a total of 6 weeks only

explanation: For MSSA prosthetic joint infection with retained prosthesis, rifampin plus an oral fluoroquinolone for 3-6 months is recommended following initial IV therapy.

question: What is the recommended empiric antibiotic regimen for suspected bacterial meningitis in a 65-year-old patient with no other significant medical history?
explanation: Ceftriaxone + vancomycin + ampicillin covers S. pneumoniae, MRSA, and Listeria, which is required for patients ≥50 years with suspected bacterial meningitis.

question: A patient with a history of previous bacterial endocarditis is scheduled for a dental procedure that involves perforation of the oral mucosa. Which prophylactic antibiotic regimen is recommended?

options.text: Amoxicillin 2 g taken 1 hour before the procedure
Clindamycin 600 mg taken 1 hour before the procedure
Amoxicillin 2 g taken immediately after the procedure
A 5-day course of cephalexin starting the day of the procedure

explanation: Amoxicillin 2 g orally 30-60 minutes before the dental procedure is the standard prophylaxis regimen for patients with previous endocarditis.

question: A patient is diagnosed with a severe, initial episode of *Clostridioides difficile* infection, indicated by a white blood cell count of 18,000 cells/mm3 and a serum creatinine of 1.6 mg/dL. Which is the recommended first-line treatment?

options.text: Oral vancomycin 125 mg four times daily for 10 days
Rifaximin 400 mg three times daily for 20 days
Oral metronidazole 500 mg three times daily for 10 days

explanation: Oral vancomycin 125 mg four times daily for 10 days is recommended for severe CDI (WBC ≥15,000 or SCr >1.5 mg/dL).

question: According to the principles of surgical prophylaxis, when is the optimal time to administer a preoperative dose of cefazolin?

options.text: On call to the operating room
Immediately after the surgical incision is closed
Within 60 minutes before the surgical incision
2 to 4 hours before the surgical incision

explanation: Cefazolin should be administered within 60 minutes before the surgical incision to ensure adequate tissue levels at the time of incision.

question: According to current guidelines, when should antiretroviral therapy (ART) be initiated for a newly diagnosed, asymptomatic HIV-positive patient with a CD4 count of 600 cells/mm3?

options.text: When the patient develops an AIDS-defining illness
When the CD4 count falls below 500 cells/mm3
Immediately, as treatment is recommended for all infected patients regardless of CD4 count
When the CD4 count falls below 350 cells/mm3

explanation: ART is recommended immediately for all HIV-infected patients regardless of CD4 count per current DHHS guidelines.

question: A patient with HIV has a CD4 count of 150 cells/mm3. What is the primary opportunistic infection for which prophylaxis is indicated at this stage?
explanation: At CD4 150 cells/mm³ (below 200), primary prophylaxis for PJP (Pneumocystis jiroveci pneumonia) is indicated.

question: A patient without HIV is diagnosed with drug-susceptible active pulmonary tuberculosis. What is the standard duration of the continuation phase of treatment, following a 2-month intensive phase?
explanation: The continuation phase of standard TB treatment is 4 months (isoniazid + rifampin), following the 2-month intensive phase.

question: A neutropenic patient is diagnosed with invasive aspergillosis. Which antifungal agent is considered the preferred first-line therapy?
explanation: Voriconazole is the preferred first-line therapy for invasive aspergillosis per IDSA guidelines.

question: A patient is diagnosed with onychomycosis of the toenails. Which oral therapy and duration are most appropriate for treatment?
explanation: Terbinafine for 12 weeks is the preferred treatment for toenail onychomycosis — more effective and better tolerated than alternatives.

question: Hospital-Acquired Pneumonia (HAP) is formally defined as pneumonia occurring at least how long after a patient’s admission to the hospital?
explanation: HAP is defined as pneumonia in a nonventilated patient that occurs 48 hours or more after admission and was not incubating at the time of admission.

question: Which finding is considered a Major Criterion in the IDSA/ATS criteria for defining Severe Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAP)?
explanation: The major criteria for severe CAP include septic shock with the need for vasopressors or respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation. Confusion, leukopenia, and respiratory rate > 30 breaths/min are considered minor criteria.

question: For Influenza treatment to resolve symptoms 1 to 1.5 days sooner, Neuraminidase inhibitors (e.g., Oseltamivir) must be initiated within how many hours of symptom onset?
explanation: Neuraminidase inhibitors must be initiated within 48 hours of symptom onset for treatment benefits.

question: Which first-line agent for uncomplicated cystitis is specifically noted as being ineffective if the patient's creatinine clearance (CrCl) is less than 30 mL/minute/1.73 m²?
explanation: Nitrofurantoin (monohydrate/macrocrystals) is ineffective in patients with a CrCl less than 30 mL/minute/1.73 m².

question: What is the minimum recommended duration of antibiotic therapy for acute bacterial prostatitis?
explanation: The therapy duration for acute bacterial prostatitis is typically 2–4 weeks, and may be extended for an additional 2 weeks if the patient remains symptomatic.

question: Which physical finding is characteristic of Erysipelas, helping to differentiate it from cellulitis?

options.text: Involvement primarily of the deep dermis and subcutaneous fat
Edge of infection is elevated and sharply demarcated from the surrounding tissue
Warmth, pain, and non-elevated, poorly defined margins
Usually caused by a mixed infection with Gram-positive and Gram-negative organisms

explanation: Erysipelas involves the superficial dermis and the edge of the infection is elevated and sharply demarcated from the surrounding tissue. Cellulitis involves the deep dermis/subcutaneous fat and has non-elevated, poorly defined margins.

question: If Group A Streptococcus or Clostridium spp. are suspected in a patient with necrotizing fasciitis, which agent should be included in the empiric regimen specifically to suppress toxin and cytokine production?
explanation: If S. pyogenes (Group A streptococci) or Clostridium spp. are suspected, clindamycin or linezolid should be included in the empiric regimen to suppress toxin and cytokine production.

question: What is the recommended strategy for antibiotic treatment following the initial intravenous therapy phase for a prosthetic joint infection caused by S. aureus where the prosthesis is retained?

options.text: Oral Nafcillin or Dicloxacillin monotherapy for 1 month
Rifampin combined with a fluoroquinolone for 3 to 6 months
Oral clindamycin plus doxycycline for 12 weeks

explanation: For retained staphylococcal prosthetic joint infections, the pathogen-specific intravenous therapy plus rifampin for 2–6 weeks is followed by rifampin plus ciprofloxacin or levofloxacin for 3 months (hip, elbow, shoulder, ankle) or 6 months (retained knee prosthesis).

question: Which criterion is explicitly required for transitioning a patient with osteomyelitis from parenteral (IV) to oral antibiotic therapy?

options.text: Patient must have been stable for at least 7 days on IV therapy
C-reactive protein must be less than 2.0 mg/dL
The oral antibiotic chosen must be a macrolide
The patient must have completed all necessary surgical debridement

explanation: Criteria for effective oral therapy for osteomyelitis include a highly bioavailable antibiotic, documented adherence, adequate surgical debridement, resolving clinical course, and a C-reactive protein less than 2.0 mg/dL.

question: What unique antibiotic must be included in the empiric meningitis regimen for older adults (age 50 years and older) to ensure coverage for Listeria monocytogenes?
explanation: For older adults (50 years and older), the empiric regimen is Ceftriaxone plus Vancomycin plus Ampicillin, where Ampicillin covers Listeria monocytogenes.

question: Adjunctive Dexamethasone therapy is recommended for adult patients with meningitis, particularly to improve outcomes if the meningitis is caused by which pathogen?
explanation: Dexamethasone is recommended because it resulted in significantly improved outcomes, including decreased mortality, in adults receiving it for S. pneumoniae meningitis.

question: What is the recommended minimum duration of Nafcillin or Cefazolin therapy for native valve endocarditis caused by methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA)?
explanation: For native valve endocarditis caused by MSSA, the recommended length of therapy using Oxacillin, Nafcillin, or Cefazolin is 6 weeks.

question: What is the standard antibiotic regimen for endocarditis prophylaxis in an adult patient with a prosthetic heart valve who is undergoing a dental procedure involving gingival tissue?

options.text: Clindamycin 300 mg orally 1 hour before procedure
Cephalexin 500 mg orally 1 hour before procedure
Amoxicillin 2 g orally 1 hour before procedure
Ampicillin 2 g intravenously 1 hour before procedure

explanation: Patients with prosthetic valves require prophylaxis for dental procedures. The Standard general prophylaxis regimen is Amoxicillin 2 g, 1 hour before the procedure.

question: What is the recommended duration of antibiotic therapy for secondary peritonitis following adequate source control (e.g., surgical repair)?
explanation: The recommended therapy duration for secondary peritonitis is 4 days following source control.

question: Which finding meets the criteria for defining a severe initial episode of Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI)?

options.text: Albumin 2.8 g/dL (not listed as specific severity measure)

explanation: Severe CDI is defined by leukocytosis with WBC 15×103 cells/mm³ or greater or SCr greater than 1.5 mg/dL.

question: For a surgical procedure using Cefazolin for prophylaxis, when must an additional intraoperative dose be administered?

options.text: If the procedure lasts longer than 1 hour
If the initial dose was given more than 2 hours before incision
If the patient weighs more than 120 kg
If the surgery lasts longer than 4 hours or involves considerable blood loss

explanation: The antibiotic must be redosed if the surgery lasts longer than 4 hours (or more than two drug half-lives) or involves considerable blood loss.

question: What is the goal of treatment (viral suppression) for an HIV-infected patient receiving combination ART?

options.text: CD4 T-cell count greater than 200 cells/mm³
HIV RNA below the level of detection of the assay (typically < 20 copies/mL to 75 copies/mL)
A 50% decrease in HIV RNA from baseline

explanation: Viral suppression, which is the goal of treatment, is defined as HIV RNA below the level of detection of the assay (HIV RNA less than 20 copies/mL to 75 copies/mL).

question: Which ART component is associated with a benign increase in serum creatinine due to the inhibition of creatinine secretion, but is not generally considered nephrotoxic?
explanation: Dolutegravir and Elvitegravir/Cobicistat (INSTIs) cause benign increases in creatinine because they inhibit creatinine secretion.

question: Secondary prophylaxis (chronic maintenance therapy) for Cytomegalovirus (CMV) retinitis may be discontinued if the CMV lesions are inactive and the patient’s CD4 count has remained above 100 cells/mm³ for at least how long in response to ART?
explanation: Chronic maintenance therapy for CMV retinitis may be discontinued if the lesions are inactive, and the CD4+ count is greater than 100 cells/mm³ for 3–6 months in response to ART.

question: Which Rifamycin is substituted for Rifampin when treating active TB in a patient whose ART regimen includes a ritonavir-boosted Protease Inhibitor (PI)?
explanation: PIs should not be administered concurrently with rifampin. Rifabutin can be substituted for rifampin; if the PI is boosted with ritonavir, the rifabutin dose should be decreased to 150 mg every day.

question: What is the preferred prophylactic regimen for an HIV-infected patient requiring PJP primary prophylaxis (CD4 < 200 cells/mm³) who has a known sulfonamide allergy?

options.text: Pentamidine 300 mg by nebulization once monthly

explanation: Trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole is the preferred agent, but due to a sulfonamide allergy, the preferred alternative prophylactic regimen based on safety, tolerability, and efficacy is Atovaquone 1500 mg once daily.

question: What is the preferred first-line agent for the treatment of Aspergillosis?
explanation: Voriconazole is listed as the first therapeutic option for Aspergillosis.

question: Which medication is currently noted as the preferred first-line oral therapy for nonhospitalized, symptomatic patients diagnosed with COVID-19, as of January 2023?

options.text: Remdesivir 200 mg once, then 100 mg daily

explanation: Ritonavir-boosted nirmatrelvir (Paxlovid) is preferred first-line therapy for symptomatic, nonhospitalized patients with COVID-19 who are not receiving supplemental oxygen, as of January 2023.

question: A patient is diagnosed with bacterial sinusitis that is considered severe. What is the recommended duration of antibiotic therapy for this patient, assuming they are an adult?
explanation: The therapy duration for acute bacterial sinusitis in adults is 5–7 days. Children require 10–14 days of therapy.

question: In a hospital unit where the prevalence of MRSA respiratory infections is low (less than 10%), what is the clinical implication of a negative MRSA nasal screen in a patient with suspected VAP?

options.text: The patient must still receive Vancomycin for the first 48 hours empirically.
The MRSA nasal screen is inconclusive and should be repeated immediately.
The negative screen suggests that empiric MRSA antibiotic coverage is unnecessary.
The patient should receive Linezolid, regardless of the nasal screen result.

explanation: In locations where the prevalence of MRSA respiratory infections is low (< 10%), a negative MRSA nasal screen suggests that MRSA antibiotics are unnecessary empirically.

question: When comparing the symptoms of Influenza, COVID-19, and the Common Cold, which symptom is characteristic of Influenza, typically lasting 3–4 days?
explanation: High temperature (> 101°F [38°C]) of 3–4 days’ duration is a characteristic symptom listed for Influenza.

question: Which molecular class of β-Lactamases is noted in the sources as currently having no effective inhibitors available?

options.text: Class D (Serine β-lactamases, e.g., OXA family)

explanation: Metallo β-lactamases (Molecular Class B), which include the IMP and VIM families (CRE), are listed as having None under the "Effective Inhibitors" category.

question: For an HIV-infected patient receiving ART, a change in HIV RNA (viral load) is considered statistically significant if the change is greater than:
explanation: While on treatment, changes greater than 3-fold (about 0.5 log) in HIV RNA are considered statistically significant.

question: Which mechanism of antibiotic resistance is characteristic of glycopeptide resistance (e.g., vancomycin resistance) in Enterococcus species?

options.text: Altered target site (e.g., vanA and vanB genes)

explanation: Glycopeptides (vancomycin) resistance is caused by an Altered target site (e.g., vanA and vanB genes).

question: Which description best characterizes the CSF findings typically associated with Viral Meningitis, differentiating it from Bacterial Meningitis?

options.text: Glucose less than 50 mg/dL and protein greater than 150 mg/dL.

explanation: Viral Meningitis typically shows 5–300 WBCs/mm³ (predominantly lymphocytes). Options A, C, and D are characteristic of Bacterial Meningitis.

question: What is the recommended duration of antibiotic therapy for native valve endocarditis caused by Penicillin-susceptible oral streptococci, using Penicillin G monotherapy?
explanation: The length of therapy for Penicillin-susceptible oral streptococci native valve endocarditis using Penicillin G (PCN G) is 4 weeks.

question: What is the preferred initial antifungal regimen for a non-neutropenic patient with candidemia (bloodstream Candida infection)?
explanation: The preferred initial therapy for candidemia is an Echinocandin (caspofungin, anidulafungin, rezafungin, or micafungin). Fluconazole is only used if the organism is known or suspected to be susceptible.

question: Which intervention is mandatory to reduce the bone marrow–suppressive effects of pyrimethamine when treating Toxoplasmosis?
explanation: When treating toxoplasmosis, folinic acid (leucovorin) (10–25 mg/day) must be added to the regimen (Pyrimethamine plus Sulfadiazine) to reduce bone marrow–suppressive effects of pyrimethamine.

question: When initiating combination ART in an HIV-infected pregnant woman, which class of antibiotics is generally avoided during the entire pregnancy due to safety concerns?
explanation: Although the source context is UTI treatment in pregnancy, it lists Tetracyclines (along with Fluoroquinolones and Aminoglycosides) as antibiotics to avoid during pregnancy. (Note: While ART is used to prevent perinatal transmission, tetracyclines are a general contraindication in pregnancy.)

question: Which combination ART agent causes a benign increase in serum creatinine by inhibiting creatinine secretion, but is not considered nephrotoxic?
explanation: Bictegravir (as well as Dolutegravir and Elvitegravir/Cobicistat) causes benign increases in creatinine because it inhibits creatinine secretion. TDF is associated with renal toxicity.

question: What is the primary goal established by the CDC regarding the prevention of perinatal HIV transmission?

options.text: Ensuring all HIV-infected pregnant women receive Zidovudine.
Decreasing transmissions to less than 1% through suppressive combination ART.
Ensuring the infant receives Zidovudine for at least 6 weeks postpartum.
Initiating ART only if the maternal CD4 count is below 200 cells/mm³.

explanation: The goal of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention is to decrease transmissions to less than 1% through suppressive combination antiretroviral therapy (ART) administered throughout pregnancy.

question: What is the standard total duration of therapy recommended for pulmonary tuberculosis (TB) in a patient without HIV who has cavitary lesions on a chest x-ray and a positive sputum culture after completing the 2-month intensive phase?
explanation: If a patient without HIV has pulmonary TB and the culture is positive after 2 months of TB treatment, the total duration should be extended to 9 months. (The standard duration is 6 months; this extension is triggered by the positive culture after the intensive phase).

question: Which of the following is an accepted alternative regimen for the treatment of latent TB infection (LTBI) in a patient who is not infected with HIV?

options.text: Rifapentine plus isoniazid daily for 6 months

explanation: Recommended dosing regimens for LTBI in non-HIV infected patients include Rifampin 600 mg daily for 4 months.

question: Which characteristic differentiates Influenza (Flu) from the Common Cold, according to the sources?

options.text: Presence of a dry cough that can become severe

explanation: High temperature (greater than 101∘F [38∘C]) of 3–4 days’ duration is listed as characteristic of Influenza, while temperature is only occasional in the common cold.

question: Empiric treatment for hospitalized patients with non-severe Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAP) should include a β-Lactam (such as Ceftriaxone or Ampicillin/sulbactam) plus which other class of antibiotic?
explanation: Empiric treatment of hospitalized patients with non-severe pneumonia includes a β-Lactam (ampicillin/sulbactam, ceftriaxone, or ceftaroline) plus a macrolide (or doxycycline).

question: In a hospital unit where the incidence of MRSA respiratory infections is greater than 20%, empiric therapy for Hospital-Acquired Pneumonia (HAP) or Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia (VAP) must include which agent?
explanation: An antibiotic with activity against MRSA should be included if MRSA incidence in the hospital unit is greater than 20%. The options if MRSA activity is necessary are Vancomycin or Linezolid.

question: What is the recommended duration for the use of Rifampin in the regimen for treating Latent TB Infection (LTBI) in a patient who is not coinfected with HIV?
explanation: For patients who are not infected with HIV, one recommended dosing regimen is Rifampin 600 mg daily for 4 months.

question: In bacterial meningitis, which of the following cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) lab results is characteristic of the infection?
explanation: Bacterial meningitis typically results in WBC greater than 1000–5000 cells/mm³ (predominantly neutrophils), protein greater than 150 mg/dL, glucose less than 50 mg/dL, and lactic acid greater than 35 mg/dL.

question: Which drug is used for chemoprophylaxis against N. meningitidis in adults who are identified as close contacts of an index case?

options.text: Rifampin 300 mg every 12 hours for four doses

explanation: Ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally, one dose, is listed as a chemoprophylaxis option for adults who are close contacts of an N. meningitidis case. (Note: Rifampin dosing for adults is 600 mg every 12 hours, four doses.)

question: When treating a brain abscess that originated from trauma or neurosurgery, which combination of antibiotics is suggested for the empiric regimen?
explanation: Suggested empiric regimens for a brain abscess secondary to Trauma or Neurosurgery is Vancomycin plus Cefepime.

question: For a total knee replacement lasting longer than 4 hours, what is the required adjustment to the antibiotic prophylaxis regimen?

options.text: Switch from Cefazolin to Vancomycin due to the longer duration.
Increase the initial dose of Cefazolin by 50%.
Give antibiotics for 24 hours postoperatively instead of stopping at surgical closure.

explanation: Prophylaxis principles require that the antibiotic must be redosed if the surgery lasts longer than 4 hours (or more than two half-lives of the antibiotic).

question: For necrotizing fasciitis caused by S. pyogenes (Group A Streptococcus), the definitive treatment includes high-dose intravenous penicillin plus which other agent?
explanation: Streptococcal necrotizing fasciitis treatment is high-dose intravenous penicillin plus clindamycin or linezolid.

question: What is the recommended duration of therapy for prosthetic valve endocarditis caused by Penicillin-susceptible oral streptococci?
explanation: The length of therapy for prosthetic valve endocarditis caused by Penicillin-susceptible oral streptococci is 6 weeks.

question: Which opportunistic infection (OI) in an HIV patient with a low CD4 count (less than 50 cells/mm³) requires regular funduscopic examinations rather than primary pharmacological prophylaxis?
explanation: Primary prophylaxis is not recommended for CMV retinitis; instead, in patients with CD4 counts less than 50 cells/mm³, regular funduscopic examinations are recommended.

question: When treating severe Histoplasmosis, what is the recommended duration for the initial induction phase using Amphotericin B lipid formulation?
explanation: For moderate to severe Histoplasmosis, the treatment involves Lipid formulation amphotericin B for at least 1–2 weeks followed by Itraconazole.

question: Which combination ART agent must be avoided in patients with a history of using long-acting cabotegravir (CAB-LA) for pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP)?
explanation: The PI-based regimen Darunavir/ritonavir or darunavir/cobicistat plus tenofovir/emtricitabine is listed with the caution: "For people with HIV who do not have a history of using long-acting cabotegravir (CAB-LA) as pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP)".

question: What is the recommended treatment duration for uncomplicated cystitis using Trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole?
explanation: Trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole (160 mg/800 mg twice daily) is recommended for 3 days for uncomplicated cystitis.

question: Which risk factor for postoperative wound infection requires only 102 microorganisms to cause infection, compared to the 105 inoculum needed in a healthy person?
explanation: A healthy person tolerates an inoculum of 105 microorganisms, but in the presence of a foreign body, only 102 are needed to cause infection.

question: Which molecular class of β-Lactamases, which includes IMP and VIM family enzymes, is currently noted in the sources as having no effective inhibitors available?
explanation: Metallo β-lactamases (Molecular Class B), exemplified by the IMP and VIM family, are listed as having None under "Effective Inhibitors".

question: Which mechanism of resistance against β-Lactam antibiotics is mediated by the expression of the mecA gene in Staphylococcus species?
explanation: Resistance to β-Lactams through mechanisms like altered penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs) and the mecA gene is classified as an Altered target site resistance mechanism.

question: In a patient receiving combination ART, a change in HIV viral load is considered statistically significant if the change is greater than:
explanation: While on treatment, changes greater than 3-fold (about 0.5 log) in HIV RNA are considered statistically significant.

question: What is the recommended duration of antibiotic therapy for uncomplicated pyelonephritis when treated with Levofloxacin (750 mg orally daily) in an outpatient setting?
explanation: Levofloxacin 750 mg orally daily for uncomplicated pyelonephritis is a 5-day duration. Ciprofloxacin requires 7 days, and trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole requires 14 days.

question: What is the standard duration of therapy for acute bacterial prostatitis?
explanation: Therapy duration for acute bacterial prostatitis is typically 2–4 weeks, which can be extended for an additional 2 weeks if the patient remains symptomatic.

question: Which condition is a contraindication for the use of Nitrofurantoin in the treatment of uncomplicated cystitis?

options.text: Creatinine clearance (CrCl) less than 30 mL/minute/1.73 m²
Prior use of a β-Lactam antibiotic

explanation: Nitrofurantoin monohydrate/macrocrystals is noted as ineffective in patients with a CrCl less than 30 mL/minute/1.73 m².

question: Which definitive treatment regimen is indicated for S.pneumoniae meningitis when the pathogen exhibits a Penicillin MIC of 2.0 mcg/mL or greater (highly resistant)?
explanation: If the Penicillin MIC is 2.0 mcg/mL or greater, the regimen is Vancomycin plus Ceftriaxone.

question: Which regimen is required for long-term oral suppression following the initial intravenous therapy for a prosthetic knee infection caused by Staphylococcus that is retained?

options.text: Rifampin plus a fluoroquinolone for 6 months

explanation: If a prosthetic joint is retained, the oral continuation phase is rifampin plus ciprofloxacin or levofloxacin for 3 months (hip, elbow, shoulder, ankle) or 6 months (retained knee prosthesis).

question: In the empiric treatment of a brain abscess secondary to Trauma or Neurosurgery, what is the core antibiotic combination?
explanation: The suggested empiric regimen for a brain abscess secondary to Trauma or Neurosurgery is Vancomycin plus Cefepime.

question: What is the required prophylactic dose of Cefazolin for a patient weighing 135 kg undergoing total knee replacement?
explanation: The appropriate prophylactic dosing for Cefazolin is 2 g, or 3 g for patients weighing 120 kg or more.

question: For a dental procedure involving gingival tissue in a patient with a previous history of bacterial endocarditis, which oral regimen is recommended for prophylaxis?

options.text: Cephalexin 2 g orally 1 hour before procedure
Azithromycin 500 mg orally 1 hour before procedure
Amoxicillin 2 g orally 1 hour before procedure
Amoxicillin 3 g orally 1 hour before procedure

explanation: Standard general prophylaxis for dental procedures is Amoxicillin 2 g, 1 hour before the procedure.

question: When treating native valve endocarditis caused by methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA), what is the mandatory length of therapy using Cefazolin?
explanation: For MSSA native valve endocarditis, the length of therapy using Oxacillin, Nafcillin, or Cefazolin is 6 weeks.

question: Which agent is the preferred initial therapy for candidemia in a non-neutropenic patient, prior to known species identification or susceptibility testing?
explanation: The preferred initial therapy for candidemia is an Echinocandin (caspofungin, anidulafungin, rezafungin, or micafungin).

question: What is the required prophylactic co-treatment when using Pyrimethamine to treat Toxoplasmosis, due to the need to reduce bone marrow suppression?
explanation: You must Add folinic acid (leucovorin) 10–25 mg/day to reduce bone marrow–suppressive effects of pyrimethamine.

question: Which is the most appropriate minimum duration of therapy for moderate to severe Histoplasmosis?

options.text: 1–2 weeks of IV therapy followed by oral therapy for 12 weeks total

explanation: Moderate to severe illness requires Lipid formulation amphotericin B for at least 1–2 weeks followed by itraconazole to complete 12 weeks of therapy.

question: Which antiretroviral agent causes a benign increase in serum creatinine by inhibiting creatinine secretion, but is not considered nephrotoxic?
explanation: Dolutegravir (and other INSTIs like Bictegravir and Elvitegravir/Cobicistat) cause benign increases in creatinine because they inhibit creatinine secretion. TDF is associated with renal toxicity.

question: What is the required adjunct medication for all patients receiving Isoniazid for Tuberculosis (TB) treatment who are at increased risk of neuropathy (e.g., HIV-infected persons, patients with diabetes, or alcoholism)?
explanation: Pyridoxine should be given to all those receiving isoniazid and at increased risk of neuropathy (e.g., pregnant women, breastfeeding infants, HIV infected persons, patients with diabetes, alcoholism, malnutrition, or chronic renal failure, persons of advanced age).

question: Secondary prophylaxis (chronic maintenance therapy) for Mycobacterium avium Complex (MAC) can be discontinued if the patient has completed 12 months of therapy and their CD4 count has remained above:
explanation: Chronic maintenance therapy for MAC may be discontinued after 12 months of therapy if CD4 count is more than 100 cells/mm³ for 6 months or longer because of potent combination ART

question: A 24-year-old pregnant woman presents to the urgent care clinic with fever and urinary frequency and urgency. She is diagnosed with a urinary tract infection (UTI). Based on potential harm to the fetus, which of the following medications should be avoided in treating her UTI?
explanation: Doxycycline (a tetracycline) should be avoided due to the potential harm to the fetus. Nitrofurantoin, amoxicillin (a penicillin), and cephalexin (a cephalosporin) are generally considered safe.

question: Which of the following is the primary method of β-lactam resistance with Streptococcus pneumoniae?

options.text: Decreased drug levels due to changes in permeability
Decreased drug levels due to an efflux pump

explanation: S. pneumoniae resistance to β-lactam antibiotics involves alteration in one or more of the major penicillin-binding proteins.

question: Which of the following agents is considered a narrow-spectrum antibiotic?
explanation: Isoniazid is only active against Mycobacterium tuberculosis, while ceftriaxone, ciprofloxacin, and imipenem are considered broad spectrum due to their activity against multiple types of bacteria and increased risk for contributing to the development of a superinfection.

question: Which of the following antibiotics exhibits concentration-dependent killing?
explanation: Clindamycin, linezolid, and vancomycin exhibit time-dependent killing, while daptomycin works best when administered in a fashion that optimizes concentration-dependent killing.

question: Which of the following antibiotics exhibits a long postantibiotic effect that permits once-daily dosing?
explanation: Aminoglycosides, including gentamicin, possess a long postantibiotic effect, especially when given as a high dose every 24 hours. Penicillin G, clindamycin, and vancomycin have a relatively short postantibiotic effect and require dosing that maintains concentrations above the MIC for a longer portion of the dosing interval.

question: A 58-year-old man with a history of hepatitis C, cirrhosis, and ascites presents with spontaneous bacterial peritonitis. Which of the following antibiotics requires close monitoring and dosing adjustment in this patient given his liver disease?
explanation: Erythromycin is metabolized by the liver and should be used with caution in patients with hepatic impairment. Penicillin G, tobramycin, and vancomycin are primarily eliminated by the kidneys.

question: JS is a 3-day-old neonate, born at 37 weeks’ gestation, who presents with new onset fever, lethargy, and decreased desire to feed. Based on JS’s age, which of the following antibiotics is considered safe to use in neonates?
explanation: Chloramphenicol and sulfonamides (sulfamethoxazole) can cause toxic effects in newborns due to poorly developed renal and hepatic elimination processes. Tetracycline can have effects on bone growth and development and should be avoided in newborns and young children. Ampicillin is safe and effective in this population.

question: When evaluating drug therapy for meningitis, which of the following factors is expected to have the LEAST influence on the penetration and concentration of an antibacterial agent in the cerebrospinal fluid?

options.text: Minimum inhibitory concentration of the drug

explanation: Although the minimum inhibitory concentration impacts the effectiveness of the drug against a given bacteria, it does not affect the ability of a drug to penetrate into the brain. Lipid solubility, protein binding, and molecular weight all determine the likelihood of a drug to penetrate the blood–brain barrier and concentrate in the brain.

question: A 72-year-old male presents with fever, cough, malaise, and shortness of breath. His chest x-ray shows bilateral infiltrates consistent with pneumonia. Bronchial wash cultures reveal Pseudomonas aeruginosa sensitive to cefepime. Which of the following is the best dosing scheme for cefepime based on the drug's time-dependent bactericidal activity?

options.text: 1 g every 6 hours given over 30 minutes
2 g every 12 hours given over 3 hours
4 g every 24 hours given over 30 minutes
4 g given as continuous infusion over 24 hours

explanation: The clinical efficacy of cefepime is based on the percentage of time that the drug concentration remains above the MIC. A continuous infusion would allow for the greatest amount of time above the MIC compared to intermittent (30 minutes) and prolonged infusions (3 to 4 hours).

question: Which of the following adverse drug reactions precludes a patient from being rechallenged with that drug in the future?
explanation: Stevens-Johnson syndrome is a severe idiosyncratic reaction that can be life threatening, and these patients should never be rechallenged with the offending agent. Itching/rash is a commonly reported reaction in patients receiving penicillins but is not life threatening. A patient may be rechallenged if the benefits outweigh the risk (for example, pregnant patient with syphilis) or the patient could be exposed through a desensitization procedure. GI upset is a common side effect of clarithromycin but is not due to an allergic reaction. Moxifloxacin is a broad-spectrum antibiotic that can inhibit the normal flora of the GI tract, increasing the risk for the development of superinfections like C. difficile. This is not an allergic reaction, and the patient can be rechallenged; however, the patient might be at risk for developing C. difficile infection again.

question: A 45-year-old man presented to the hospital 3 days ago with severe cellulitis and a large abscess on his left leg. Incision and drainage were performed on the abscess, and cultures revealed methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus. Which is the most appropriate treatment option for once-daily outpatient intravenous therapy in this patient?
explanation: Daptomycin is approved for skin and skin structure infections caused by MRSA and is given once daily. A and D are incorrect because they do not cover MRSA. Ceftaroline covers MRSA, but it must be given twice daily.

question: Which of the following adverse effects is associated with daptomycin?
